content.assessment: This proposal aligns partially with the Finnish comprehensive-school logic by targeting investment in comprehensive schools as a precondition for structural change, echoing the complementary reforms (teacher qualifications, curriculum) that accompanied Finland's 1972-1977 transition and contributed to its eventual success (F-live-finnish_reform-05). However, the Finnish case also demonstrates a critical limitation: quality investment in comprehensive schools was necessary but not sufficient—Finland still required actual structural unification to achieve the variance reductions observed by the 1980s-1990s. Quality investment alone, without addressing tracking age, does not directly correspond to any lens variable that produced Finland's measured effects, since Finland's reform bundled structural and quality dimensions simultaneously rather than sequencing quality-first.
opportunities: If eventually paired with structural reform, could replicate the bundled approach that made Finland's reform effective, per the two-decade timeline observed there
strengths: Investment in comprehensive-school quality parallels one component of Finland's successful bundled reform (teacher qualifications, curriculum redesign) (F-live-finnish_reform-05)
threats: Even after comprehensive quality improvements are achieved, Finland's later experience shows persistent risk of SES gap resurgence via within-school selection and school-choice effects, suggesting quality investment without structural safeguards may not be durable (F-live-finnish_reform-06)
weaknesses: Finland's evidence suggests quality investment was one part of a simultaneous structural-and-quality reform package, not a standalone precondition-only strategy; the proposal's sequencing logic (quality first, structure later) has no direct support in the Finnish case
Does not address tracking age at all, leaving the primary inequality-associated mechanism identified in cross-national evidence unchanged (F-live-finnish_reform-04)
